segments.text:
Protic Acids and Bases
Understanding the behavior of acids and bases is essential to every branch of science related to chemistry.
In aqueous chemistry:
An acid increases the concentration of H 3 O + when added to water.
A base decreases the concentration of H 3 O + when added to water.
As H 3 O + decreases in solution, OH − increases. K W = [H 3 O + ][OH − ]
Protic refers to chemistry involving transfer of an H + from one molecule to another.

Brønsted -Lowry Acids and Bases
An acid is a proton donor.
A base is a proton acceptor.
A salt contains cations and anions.
Strong electrolytes dissociate nearly completely into ions in dilute aqueous solutions.
Conjugate Acids and Bases

The Nature of H + and OH − (1 of 2)
The proton does not exist by itself in water; the simplest formula is the hydronium ion , H 3 O + .
In aqeous environments, three H 2 O molecules are bound to H 3 O + via strong hydrogen bonds.
Figure 6-4
Figure 6-5

The Nature of H + and OH − (2 of 2)
Water as an acid
(proton donor)
Water as a base
(proton acceptor)

Autoprotolysis (self-ionization)
Water undergoes autoprotolysis in which it acts as both acid and base.
(6-17a)
(6-17b)
All protic solvents undergo autoprotolysis .
(6-18)

Autoprotolysis Constant, K w
The equilibrium constant for autoprotolysis has a special symbol, K w .
H 2 O
H + + OH − K W = [H + ][OH − ]
K w varies with temperature (Table 6-1)
At 25.0°C K W = 1.01 × 10 −14
pH = −log[H + ]
pH + pOH = −log( K W ) = 14.00 at 25°C

pH Scale
Acidic solutions have:
[H + ] > [OH − ]
pH < 7
Basic solutions have:
[H + ] < [OH − ]
pH > 7
Figure 6-8
Generally pH = 0–14
However, these are not the limits of pH
Very high concentrations of acid can reach pH = −1

Example: Concentration of H + and OH − in Pure Water at 25°C
Que: Calculate the concentrations of H + and OH − in pure water at 25°C.
Solution: The stoichiometry of Reaction 6-19 tells us that H + and OH − are produced in a 1:1 molar ratio. Their concentrations must be equal. Calling each concentration x , we can write
The concentrations of H + and OH − are both 1.0 × 10 −7 M in pure water.

Strong Acids and Bases
In aqueous solutions, acids and bases are commonly classified in one of two ways.
Strong acids/bases react nearly “completely” to produce H + /OH − .
Weak acids/bases react only “partially” to produce H + /OH − .
There is no sharp distinction between strong and weak.
For strong acids/bases the equilibrium dissociation constants are large.
Virtually no undissociated  strong acid/base exists in solution.
Memorize the compounds classified as strong (Table 6-2); by convention everything else is weak.
Table 6-2 Common strong acids and bases

Table 6-3 Equilibria of alkaline earth metal hydroxides
M(OH) 2 ( s )
M 2+ + 2OH −
K sp = [M 2+ ][OH − ] 2
M 2+ + OH −
MOH +
Hydroxides of alkaline earth metals can be considered strong bases for most purposes
Far less soluble than alkali metal hydroxides
Have some tendency to form MOH +

Weak Acids and Bases (1 of 2)
All weak acids (HA) react with water by donating a proton to H 2 O.
HA + H 2 O
H 3 O + + A − (6-23)
HA
H + + A − (6-24)
The acid dissociation constant ( K a ) is the equilibrium constant for a weak acid reacting with water. K a is “small” for weak acids.

Weak Acids and Bases (2 of 2)
All weak bases (B) react with water by accepting a proton from H 2 O.
B + H 2 O
BH + + OH − (6-25)
The base hydrolysis constant ( K b ) is the equilibrium constant for a weak base reacting with water. K b is “small” for weak acids.

Common Classes of Weak Acids and Bases (1 of 2)
Acetic acid is a representative carboxylic acid.
The general formula is RCO 2 H.
Most carboxylic acids are weak acids .
Most carboxylate anions are weak bases .
K a = 1.75 × 10 −5 (6-26)

Common Classes of Weak Acids and Bases (2 of 2)
(6-27)
Amines are nitrogen-containing compounds.
The “parent” of all amines is ammonia, NH 3 .
Amines are weak bases .
Ammonium ions are weak acids .
Ammonium ions

Box 6-3 The Strange Behavior of Hydrofluoric Acid
The hydrogen halides HCl, HBr, and HI are all strong acids.
So why does HF behave as a weak acid?
Fluoride forms the strongest hydrogen bond of any ion.
HF does not behave as a strong acid because F − and H 3 O + remain   associated through a hydrogen bond.
Ion pair
Other moderate acids exist predominantly as ion pairs in aqueous solutions.
Acid dissociation constants (−log K a = p K a ) for aqueous metal ions:

Compounds that can donate or accept more than one proton

Box 6-4 Carbonic Acid (1 of 2)
Carbonic acid (H 2 CO 3 ) is formed by the reaction of carbon dioxide with water.
CO 2 ( g )
CO 2 ( aq )
The hydration of CO 2 (reaction with H 2 O) and dehydration of H 2 CO 3 are slow reactions.
K a1 = 4.46 × 10 −7
K a2 = 4.69 × 10 −11

Box 6-4 Carbonic Acid (2 of 2)
Carbonic acid (H 2 CO 3 ) is formed by the reaction of carbon dioxide with water.
Carbonic acid’s behavior as a diprotic acid appears anomalous at first because K a1 is 10 2 to 10 4 times smaller than K a of other carboxylic acids.
In fact, the value commonly given for K a1 applies to all dissolved

Example: Finding K b for the Conjugate Base (2 of 3)
K a for acetic acid is 1.75 × 10 −5 . Find K b for acetate ion.
Solution: This one is trivial: †
† In this text, we use K W = 10 −14.00 = 1.0 × 10 −14 at 25°C. The more accurate value from Table 6-1 is K W = 10 −13.995 .
For acetic acid with K a = 10 −4.756 , the accurate value of K b is
10 −(13.995−4.756) = 10 −9.239 = 5.77 × 10 −10 .
